section.title: Curl tests in the plane and in space
section.p: showed the reward for finding a potential: line integrals become endpoint differences. But finding a potential by guessing is not a method. We need a test that can say, quickly, whether a field could be conservative.
The test begins with mixed partial derivatives.

subsection.title: The test P_y=Q_x in the plane
subsection.p: Suppose \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy is exact. Then \omega=d\phi for some potential \phi. That means P=\phi_x, \qquad Q=\phi_y. Differentiate the first equation with respect to y: P_y=\phi_{xy}. Differentiate the second equation with respect to x: Q_x=\phi_{yx}. If the mixed partial derivatives are continuous, then \phi_{xy}=\phi_{yx}. So every smooth exact form must satisfy \boxed{P_y=Q_x.}

example.title: A form that passes the test

statement.p: Let \omega=(2xy+3)\,dx+(x^2+2y)\,dy. Here P(x,y)=2xy+3, \qquad Q(x,y)=x^2+2y. Then P_y=2x, \qquad Q_x=2x. The test passes. In , we found the potential \phi(x,y)=x^2y+3x+y^2.

example.title: A swirling form that fails the test

statement.p: Let \eta=-y\,dx+x\,dy. Then P=-y, \qquad Q=x. So P_y=-1, \qquad Q_x=1. The test fails. Therefore \eta is not exact on the plane.
This agrees with what we already know: \oint_{\text{unit circle}} -y\,dx+x\,dy=2\pi\ne0.

subsection.p: The difference Q_x-P_y is called the scalar curl of the plane field \mathbf F=(P,Q). So the condition P_y=Q_x is the same as \boxed{Q_x-P_y=0.}

statement.p: A C^1 1-form \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy is called closed if \boxed{Q_x-P_y=0.} Equivalently, P_y=Q_x.

subsection.p: Exact forms are closed, provided the coefficients are smooth enough. This is the first appearance of a pattern that will return later as d^2=0.

theorem.title: Exact implies closed in the plane

statement.p: Let \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy be a C^1 1-form on a region D. If \omega=d\phi for a C^2 potential \phi, then \boxed{P_y=Q_x} throughout D.

proof.p: Since \omega=d\phi, we have P=\phi_x, \qquad Q=\phi_y. Therefore P_y=\phi_{xy}, \qquad Q_x=\phi_{yx}. The mixed partial derivatives of a C^2 function are equal, so P_y=Q_x.

subsection.p: The smoothness condition is real. Without it, mixed partials can misbehave.

example.title: Why smoothness matters in the mixed-partial test

statement.p: Define \Phi(x,y)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{xy(x^2-y^2)}{x^2+y^2}, \amp (x,y)\ne(0,0),\\[2mm] 0, \amp (x,y)=(0,0).\end{cases} This function has first partial derivatives at the origin, and we may form d\Phi=\Phi_x\,dx+\Phi_y\,dy. So d\Phi is exact wherever these derivatives are defined.

But the mixed partials at the origin are not equal. First compute \Phi_x(0,y)=-y, so \Phi_{xy}(0,0)=\lim_{y\to0}\frac{\Phi_x(0,y)-\Phi_x(0,0)}{y}=\lim_{y\to0}\frac{-y}{y}=-1. Also, \Phi_y(x,0)=x, so \Phi_{yx}(0,0)=\lim_{x\to0}\frac{\Phi_y(x,0)-\Phi_y(0,0)}{x}=\lim_{x\to0}\frac{x}{x}=1. Thus \Phi_{xy}(0,0)\ne \Phi_{yx}(0,0).
The problem is not exactness. The problem is lack of sufficient smoothness. The coefficient functions of d\Phi are not C^1 near the origin.

subsection.p: On a rectangle, the curl test is not only necessary. It is sufficient.

statement.p: Let R=[a,b]\times[c,d] be a rectangle, and let \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy be a C^1 1-form on R. If \boxed{P_y=Q_x} throughout R, then \omega is exact on R.

proof.p:
Choose the lower-left corner (a,c) as a base point, and define \phi(x,y)=\int_a^x P(s,c)\,ds+\int_c^y Q(x,t)\,dt. Then \phi_y(x,y)=Q(x,y). For the x-partial derivative,
\phi_x(x,y) \amp= P(x,c)+\int_c^y Q_x(x,t)\,dt \amp= P(x,c)+\int_c^y P_y(x,t)\,dt \amp= P(x,c)+\bigl(P(x,y)-P(x,c)\bigr) \amp= P(x,y).
So d\phi=P\,dx+Q\,dy=\omega.

subsection.p: This proof also tells us why the condition works. The equality P_y=Q_x makes the two ways of building \phi compatible.

subsection.title: Curl zero in space
subsection.p: In space, a 1-form has three coefficients: \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz. If it is exact, then \omega=d\phi for some potential \phi, so P=\phi_x,\qquad Q=\phi_y,\qquad R=\phi_z. The mixed partial derivatives force three compatibility conditions: \boxed{P_y=Q_x,\qquad P_z=R_x,\qquad Q_z=R_y.}

statement.p: A C^1 1-form \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz is called closed if \boxed{\nabla\times(P,Q,R)=\mathbf 0.} Equivalently, P_y=Q_x,\qquad P_z=R_x,\qquad Q_z=R_y.

example.title: A space field with zero curl

statement.p: Let \mathbf F(x,y,z)=(yz,xz,xy). Then P=yz,\qquad Q=xz,\qquad R=xy. Compute: R_y=x,\qquad Q_z=x, P_z=y,\qquad R_x=y, and Q_x=z,\qquad P_y=z. Therefore \nabla\times\mathbf F=\mathbf 0. In fact, \mathbf F=\nabla(xyz), because d(xyz)=yz\,dx+xz\,dy+xy\,dz.

example.title: A space field with nonzero curl

statement.p:
Let \mathbf G(x,y,z)=(-y,x,0). Then P=-y,\qquad Q=x,\qquad R=0. The curl is
\nabla\times\mathbf G \amp= \left(R_y-Q_z,\, P_z-R_x,\, Q_x-P_y\right) \amp= (0-0,\ 0-0,\ 1-(-1)) \amp= (0,0,2).
So \mathbf G is not conservative on all of space. Its arrows rotate around the z-axis, and the curl sees that rotation.

subsection.p: The reverse direction needs one more geometric condition.

subsection.title: Simply connected regions
subsection.p: A curl test is local. It looks at what happens in tiny neighborhoods. But a line integral can feel the shape of the whole domain.
A region D is called simply connected if every closed curve in D can be shrunk continuously to a point while staying inside D.
For the regions in this book, this means: \boxed{\text{connected, with no holes that a closed curve can wrap around.}}
Examples of simply connected regions include:
Examples that are not simply connected include:

table.title: Regions that are not simply connected.

row.cell: a disk with a smaller disk removed

subsection.p: The hole matters because a closed curve can wrap around it and refuse to shrink to a point.

theorem.title: Curl test on simply connected regions

statement.p: Let D be an open simply connected region.
In the plane, let \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy be a C^1 1-form on D. If P_y=Q_x throughout D, then \omega is exact on D.
In space, let \mathbf F=(P,Q,R) be a C^1 vector field on D. If \nabla\times\mathbf F=\mathbf 0 throughout D, then \mathbf F is conservative on D.

subsection.p: The proof for rectangles was given above by constructing a potential directly. The full simply connected version is best understood after Green's theorem and Stokes' theorem. Those theorems explain why zero local curl prevents circulation around every closed curve, as long as no hole or singularity is hiding inside the curve.
Without the simply connected condition, the test can pass and still fail to produce a global potential.

subsection.title: Local versus global potentials
subsection.p: Return to the form \alpha=\frac{-y\,dx+x\,dy}{x^2+y^2} on the punctured plane D=\mathbb R^2\setminus\{(0,0)\}. Here P(x,y)=\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}, \qquad Q(x,y)=\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}. A calculation gives P_y=\frac{y^2-x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2}, and Q_x=\frac{y^2-x^2}{(x^2+y^2)^2}. So P_y=Q_x everywhere in the punctured plane.
The local curl test passes.

But around the unit circle, \mathbf r(t)=(\cos t,\sin t), \qquad 0\le t\le2\pi, we have \alpha=dt. Therefore \boxed{\oint_C \alpha=2\pi.} If \alpha were exact on the whole punctured plane, every closed curve integral would be zero. So \alpha is not exact on D.

figure.caption: The form \alpha=\frac{-y\,dx+x\,dy}{x^2+y^2} has zero curl away from the origin, but its integral around a loop enclosing the origin is 2\pi.

subsection.p: So what is going on?

Locally, \alpha really is a differential. On the right half-plane x\gt 0, define \theta=\arctan\left(\frac{y}{x}\right). Then \theta_x=\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}, \qquad \theta_y=\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}. Therefore \boxed{d\theta=\alpha} on the right half-plane.
The same thing works on many other smaller patches. We can choose a continuous angle function on a patch that does not wrap all the way around the origin.

But there is no single continuous angle function on the whole punctured plane. After one full counterclockwise trip around the origin, the angle has increased by 2\pi. It has not returned to its original value as an ordinary single-valued function should.
This is the difference between local and global.

p.md: \boxed{\begin{aligned} \amp\text{Zero curl gives local potentials.}\\ \amp\text{A simply connected domain lets those local potentials glue into one global potential.}\\ \amp\text{A hole can prevent the gluing.} \end{aligned}}

subsection.p: The scalar curl Q_x-P_y is therefore a local measurement. It detects tiny rotation. It cannot, by itself, see a missing point that a large loop winds around. The next chapter supplies the missing global bookkeeping: Green's theorem will add the local curl over a region and compare it to circulation around the boundary. That is where the hidden hole finally has to show itself.
